steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence opens a long parenthetical after "NATO" with an em dash and then lists three member nations using semicolons. The blank falls after the last item in that list (Bulgaria, which joined in 2004). Whatever closes the blank must correctly end the parenthetical so the main clause — "are committed to NATO's principle of collective defense" — can continue.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 2004;: A semicolon joins independent clauses or separates items in a complex list, but it cannot close a parenthetical that was opened with an em dash. The parenthetical is left open, crashing into the main clause. ✗
  • Choice B — 2004,: A comma is too weak to close an em-dash parenthetical and would create a comma splice between the parenthetical list and the main clause. ✗
  • Choice C — 2004: No punctuation at all — the parenthetical bleeds directly into the main clause with no boundary, making the sentence unreadable. ✗
  • Choice D: 2004—: A parenthetical opened with an em dash must be closed with a matching em dash. Placing the second em dash after "2004" seals the parenthetical and lets the main clause resume cleanly. ✓
That matches Choice D — 2004—.
